story:
“Yes, now and forever,” it whispers, then slips into a string of broken curses. A hooded figure stands at a window, grinning. “The time is near.” It moves through the back door like it belongs, glides up the stairs, and enters a bedroom. Breathing, soft and steady. It pulls up its hood and waits. Then it leans over the bed, one hand forward, the other drawing a cruel ceremonial knife, its hilt smeared with the word kill in old, brown blood.

It pauses. Then it screeches and slashes its own wrist. Blood spills over the sheets and its shadowed robes. The figure chants, “Blood for the Blood God,” and the room darkens. A thick mist coils around it. The gash seals itself shut as the mist tightens. The body in the bed sits up, slow and mechanical. A boy. His eyes glow red.

“The path of destruction,” the boy says in a voice too soft and too certain. He stands. The figure nods, delighted, and drifts out the door without looking back. The boy follows, silent, empty.

Through the dark forest they go, the blood moon watching, smiling. The figure mutters to itself, repeats the same line, faster and louder, “Blood for the Blood God,” like a prayer, like a laugh. They reach a clearing with a stone chair. The boy sits. The figure kneels, pulls back his hood, and reveals a face eaten by disease.

“How may I serve you, lord?” he asks.

“In death,” the boy says, eyes blazing.

The figure stumbles. “But—no—” He tries to run. The mist hardens and yanks him to his knees. A blood-red circle burns into the ground. Signs crawl up his skin and glow. Invisible hands stretch him across the circle, effortless, merciless. The darkness swallows him.

He howls as his bones tear out through his skin, snap, crumble to dust. His body bursts apart, again and again, and reforms only to be broken again. Blood jets from the glowing signs and spatters the trees. The boy watches, calm. “Just a few more times,” he says, smiling as if counting.

When the ground is slick, the boy snaps his fingers. Long, thin, shadowy hands reach up and pull the circle and the man into a black abyss. The boy leans over the edge and smiles. Countless eyes open in the dark. A huge grin blooms there and vanishes.

Something stirs. Not his. Not expected. The abyss seals itself. The shadows swing toward the boy. Another red circle scratches itself into the dirt at his feet. The stone chair turns black, etched with glowing marks. The shadows wrap him like a cloak. The blood around them lifts, at first slow, then rushing, dancing with the mist before it pours into the black.

The wind rises. The dark peels away. A tall, black silhouette shatters like glass, and the boy sits where it had been, grinning. He stands. A second body, a black torso, unfolds from his back like a shadow stepping out of him. It stretches its arms. The ground browns and withers. Leaves rot. Bark crumbles.

“I am complete,” they say together, one voice in two throats. They walk toward the city. As they pass, the world dries out and falls away.
